summary: I was living in Chicago when I enlisted in the Navy on August 1st, 1944. Before joining the service, I worked at the Mills Novelty Company in Chicago, where they made slot machines and later switched to making five inch shells during the war. Chicago was my home during those early years, and it was where I met Bob, my future husband.

I graduated from Foreman High School before I enlisted in the Navy in 1944. It was important for me to finish high school first because I always wanted to be a nurse, but I didn't want to wait the four years of college that nursing required at the time. Foreman High School was where my education journey ended before I started working and eventually joined the military.

I worked at the Mills Novelty Company before I joined the Navy. They originally made slot machines, but when the war started, they switched to making five inch shells. It was there I met Bob, who was encouraged to meet me by the other fellows at the factory. I started as a factory mail girl, and it was a memorable time before I went into service.

Bob and I met at the Merchandise Mart during a special program for Mills Novelty Company employees, probably in 1943. I was there with my mother, all dressed up, and that was when Bob realized I wasn't just a kid anymore. That meeting at the Merchandise Mart marked the beginning of our life together.

I enlisted in the Navy Hospital Corps on August 1st, 1944, because I wanted to be a nurse but didn't want to wait four years of college. In the Corps, I did nursing duties like giving injections and changing bandages, even though some nurses were upset with WAVES doing nursing paperwork. My first assignments included marking organ weights during autopsies and witnessing a baby being born, which was a lot to take in as a new recruit.

I joined the WAVES during World War II and trained at Bethesda Naval Hospital. The WAVES did a lot of the nursing work, which sometimes caused tension with the regular nurses. I held the rank of HA2 when I enlisted and was promoted to HA1 by the time I left. Serving in the WAVES allowed me to fulfill my nursing aspirations and meet many people from across the country.

My boot camp was at Bethesda Naval Hospital in Maryland, where I learned hospital skills, marching, ship silhouette identification, and had calisthenics for six weeks. During that time, I had one leave when I went to New York City, visiting the Empire State Building and dancing at the Roosevelt Hotel. Bethesda was where I started my Navy journey and training before being sent to California.

During my six weeks of boot camp at Bethesda, I had one leave when I went to New York City. I visited the Empire State Building and went dancing at the Roosevelt Hotel. Although I don't remember the details of how I got there or back, that trip was a memorable break during my training in 1944.

While on leave from my Navy training at Bethesda, I visited the Empire State Building in New York City. It was part of a special outing during that brief leave in 1944. Visiting such an iconic place was a highlight during my early Navy days.

During the same leave when I visited New York City, I went dancing at the Roosevelt Hotel. It was a fun and memorable experience amid the intensity of my Navy training in 1944. That night out was one of the few moments of leisure I had during boot camp.

After boot camp, I was stationed at Oak Knoll Naval Hospital in Oakland, California, from September 20th, 1944, until April 1946. I worked in the treatment center there, caring for wounded Marines and sailors, including a Marine named Oxentine who had severe injuries. Oak Knoll was my home base during the war, and I stayed there the entire time of my service.

I was stationed in Oakland, California, at the Oak Knoll Naval Hospital during most of my Navy service from late 1944 to 1946. It was my first time away from home, and I enjoyed seeing different parts of the country on the way there, especially the train ride through the mountains. Oakland became a significant place in my life as I cared for wounded servicemen during the war.

During my time at Oak Knoll Naval Hospital, I cared for Marines who were wounded, including one Marine named Oxentine who had severe foot and stomach injuries. It was challenging but rewarding to help him recover, and I was proud to see him regain his health. The Marines I cared for left a lasting impression on me during my Navy service.

The Navy asked if I wanted to be sent overseas, including to Pearl Harbor, but I declined because I wanted to come home and marry Bob. Pearl Harbor was mentioned as a possible duty station, but I chose to stay stateside to finish my service and start my life after the war. It was a decision based on my personal plans and future.

After the war, Bob and I lived with my parents for about a year because housing was scarce. Then my parents bought a home in Elmwood Park, Illinois, where we finished off a room in the attic and lived for another year and a half. Elmwood Park was our first home after the war and a stepping stone before moving to our own house.

After living in Elmwood Park, we bought a small house in Niles, Illinois, for $11,200. It was a cozy two-bedroom, one-bath home where we raised our three children. Niles was where we settled down and started our family life after the war.

Washington state was mentioned as one of the possible places where WAVES could be sent during the war, but I was not assigned there. I chose to stay at Oak Knoll Naval Hospital in California instead. So, while Washington was part of the broader context of Navy assignments, I did not have a direct connection to it.

Hoffman Estates was not directly mentioned in my story, but it is part of the greater Chicago area where I lived and worked before and after the war. My post-war life was centered around Illinois communities like Elmwood Park and Niles, so Hoffman Estates is part of the region I know well. It represents the suburban growth that happened after the war years.
